text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Helen Murray
North Quay Medical Centre

12 Harbour View, Sunderland, SR1 3AB
Dear Dr Helen Murray
Mr Peter Lang, DOB: 14-02-1986, Hospital No: NHS748219 NHS No. 9430027719
Flat 4, Seabreeze Court, Roker, Sunderland, SR6 0PL 

I reviewed the above patient in the Neurology Clinic by scheduled telephone consultation today, in line with his offshore work rotation. He reports a history of focal epilepsy and continues regular offshore duties interspersed with onshore periods during which we arrange these reviews. He has a personalised seizure log which he updates on return to shore. He describes current frequency as “sz xfour/wk”, which he states has been consistent over the last six weeks while offshore and unchanged since his previous review in May 2025.

He characterises typical focal impaired-awareness episodes as a rising epigastric sensation followed by behavioural arrest, lip-smacking and loss of awareness for approximately 60–90 seconds, with post-event fatigue for up to one hour. He denies recent generalised convulsions. No clear nocturnal events have been noted by his partner during his recent onshore period. He reports reliable adherence to his medications using a seven-day pill organiser when at sea.

Current antiseizure therapy: Lamotrigine 150 mg b.d. and Levetiracetam 1 g b.d. He denies rash, mood lability or diplopia. Mild sleep fragmentation is reported during night shifts, and he notes that irregular sleep offshore may precipitate episodes. Alcohol intake is minimal and confined to onshore off-duty periods. He carries an up-to-date rescue plan for the offshore medic and confirms his employer is aware of his condition and restrictions; he does not undertake high-risk deck work without a buddy system and avoids working at height.

Neurological review between episodes is otherwise unremarkable. There have been no new neurological symptoms, head injuries, or infectious illnesses. Last MRI brain (March 2024) showed no structural lesion; routine EEG (April 2024) demonstrated left temporal intermittent rhythmic slowing with occasional sharp transients, supporting a diagnosis of focal epilepsy. Bloods checked by his GP in July 2025 (U&E, LFT, FBC) were within reference ranges.

Plan: Given the stable but persisting frequency (sz xfour/wk) and his current offshore commitments, we agreed to continue the present regimen until his next onshore window in six weeks, at which point we will arrange an in-person review with repeat serum levels and a sleep-deprived EEG to reassess interictal activity. He will continue to use his seizure diary and to flag any increase in events or change in semiology to the clinic via the advice line. We reaffirmed safety measures relevant to offshore work, including sleep hygiene, hydration, and adherence, and he is content with this approach.

I will update you after the next onshore assessment. In the meantime, please let me know if there are any concerns from the primary care side.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: he describes current frequency as “sz xfour/wk”, which he states has been consistent over the last six weeks

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states the patient's current seizure frequency as “sz xfour/wk” and reiterates it in the plan as “stable but persisting frequency (sz xfour/wk)”. Interpreting 'xfour/wk' as 4 per week. No other frequencies are given that would be higher, and there is no indication of seizure freedom. Therefore, the normalized label is 4 per week.
seizure_frequency_number: he describes current frequency as “sz xfour/wk”, which he states has been consistent over the last six weeks
